title: Evaluating Imbalanced Models
concept: To measure an imbalanced model's true performance, you must look beyond raw accuracy. Evaluation metrics like Precision, Recall, and the F1-Score reveal whether the model is actually learning or just guessing the majority class.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is a key advantage of augmented oversampling over plain oversampling?

options.text: It adds variations to duplicates, preventing the model from memorizing identical pixels
It runs much faster on the CPU

exercises.explain: Yes! Augmentation introduces variety, reducing overfitting on duplicates.

cards.title: The False Positive Tradeoff
cards.body: If you adjust a model to be highly sensitive to a rare class (like tumors), it will naturally flag more healthy cases as suspicious. This is a False Positive. We need metrics that measure this exact balance between finding everything and being correct.

cards.title: Precision, Recall, & F1
cards.body:
- Precision: Out of all positive predictions, how many were actually correct? (Fewer False Positives).
- Recall: Out of all actual positive cases, how many did the model find? (Fewer False Negatives).
- F1-Score: The harmonic mean of Precision and Recall, providing a single balanced metric.

cards.title: Fishing with a net vs. a spear
cards.body: Recall is like fishing with a massive net: you catch every fish in the lake (high recall), but you also scoop up trash, seaweed, and rocks (low precision). Precision is like fishing with a spear: every strike lands a fish (high precision), but you miss many fish swimming nearby (low recall). F1-Score measures how good a fisherman you are overall.

cards.title: Defect Detection Metrics
cards.body: A factory model flags 10 parts as defective. 8 are actually cracked, 2 are normal (Precision = 80%). However, there were actually 10 cracked parts on the belt, meaning 2 defects slipped past undetected (Recall = 80%). The F1-score combines these into 80%.

cards.title: There is no 'perfect' metric
cards.body: It's a myth that you should always optimize for the highest F1-score. Depending on the task, you might prefer one over the other. In medical screening, you want high **Recall** (don't miss any tumors, even if it means more false alarms). In spam filtering, you want high **Precision** (don't send clean emails to junk, even if a few spam messages get through).

left.text:
Minimize False Positives
When false alarms are costly
Example: Spam filters, lockouts

right.text:
Minimize False Negatives
When missing a case is dangerous
Example: Cancer screening, fire alarms

exercises.prompt: Which metric measures the fraction of actual positive cases that the model successfully detected?
exercises.explain: Correct! Recall (also called sensitivity) measures what fraction of actual positive cases were caught.

exercises.prompt: True or false: F1-Score is the harmonic mean of Precision and Recall, making it a robust metric for imbalanced evaluation.
exercises.explain: True. The F1-score balances precision and recall into a single metric.

exercises.sentence: Spam filters prioritize high ___ to avoid sending important personal emails to the junk folder.
exercises.explain: High precision ensures that predicted spam is actually spam, preventing false alarms on valid emails.

exercises.prompt: If a cancer-detection model has a recall of 99% but a precision of 10%, what does this mean in practice?

options.text: It finds almost all tumors, but flags many healthy patients as suspicious, requiring follow-ups
It is highly accurate and makes zero false alarms on healthy patients
It fails to detect 90% of the tumors present in the dataset

exercises.explain: Correct! High recall / low precision means it catches almost everything but triggers many false alarms.

exercises.prompt: We have completed our study of imbalanced datasets and evaluation metrics. What is the next logical step in training CNNs?

options.text: Debugging CNN training, to identify and fix common training issues like exploding gradients or overfitting
Replacing the optimizer with raw manual weight editing
Deleting the validation set entirely to speed up epochs
Deploying the model to users without evaluating it

exercises.explain: Exactly! Now that we can evaluate fairly, we must learn how to debug training when things go wrong. Next up: Debugging CNN Training!
